questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate resistance using potential difference and current.

questions.stem: A resistor has a potential difference of 6.0 V across it and a current of 0.30 A through it. What is its resistance?
questions.question: A resistor has a potential difference of 6.0 V across it and a current of 0.30 A through it. What is its resistance?
questions.explanation: Use resistance = potential difference ÷ current. R = 6.0 ÷ 0.30 = 20 ohms.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate resistance using potential difference and current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the I-V graph for an ohmic conductor.

questions.stem: Which graph would show an ohmic conductor at constant temperature?
questions.question: Which graph would show an ohmic conductor at constant temperature?
options: A straight line through the origin.
A curve that becomes flat as potential difference increases.
A line with current only in one direction.
A horizontal line at zero current.
questions.correctAnswer: A straight line through the origin.
questions.explanation: A straight line through the origin shows that current and potential difference increase in the same ratio.

distractorRationales.text: A curve that becomes flat as potential difference increases.
distractorRationales.misconception: filament lamp confused with ohmic conductor

distractorRationales.text: A line with current only in one direction.
A horizontal line at zero current.
distractorRationales.misconception: open circuit confused with conductor behaviour

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the I-V graph for an ohmic conductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Apply direct proportionality for an ohmic conductor.

questions.stem: A fixed resistor is kept at constant temperature. The potential difference across it is doubled. What happens to the current?
questions.question: A fixed resistor is kept at constant temperature. The potential difference across it is doubled. What happens to the current?
questions.explanation: For an ohmic conductor at constant temperature, current is directly proportional to potential difference.

distractorRationales.misconception: larger potential difference confused with circuit break

questions.senNote: Checks: Apply direct proportionality for an ohmic conductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Explain the shape of a filament lamp I-V graph.

questions.stem: The I-V graph for a filament lamp curves as the potential difference increases. Why does the graph curve?
questions.question: The I-V graph for a filament lamp curves as the potential difference increases. Why does the graph curve?
options: The filament gets hotter, so its resistance increases.
The filament loses all resistance when it glows.
The current changes direction every second.
The cell runs out of charge at high potential difference.
questions.correctAnswer: The filament gets hotter, so its resistance increases.
questions.explanation: As the filament heats up, the metal ions vibrate more and the resistance increases, so current rises less quickly.

distractorRationales.text: The filament loses all resistance when it glows.
distractorRationales.misconception: hot filament resistance decreases to zero

distractorRationales.text: The current changes direction every second.
distractorRationales.misconception: alternating current confused with lamp characteristic

distractorRationales.text: The cell runs out of charge at high potential difference.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain the shape of a filament lamp I-V graph.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the current-voltage characteristic of a diode.

questions.stem: Which statement about a diode is correct?
questions.question: Which statement about a diode is correct?
options: It allows current to flow much more easily in one direction than the other.
It has the same resistance in both directions.
It stores charge like a cell.
It changes alternating current into a magnetic field.
questions.correctAnswer: It allows current to flow much more easily in one direction than the other.
questions.explanation: A diode has a low resistance in the forward direction and a very high resistance in the reverse direction.

distractorRationales.text: It has the same resistance in both directions.
It stores charge like a cell.
It changes alternating current into a magnetic field.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the current-voltage characteristic of a diode.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret reverse-bias behaviour for a diode.

questions.stem: A diode is connected in reverse bias. What current would you expect?
questions.question: A diode is connected in reverse bias. What current would you expect?
options: A very small current, close to zero.
A larger current than in forward bias.
The same current as a fixed resistor.
A current that increases linearly with negative potential difference.
questions.correctAnswer: A very small current, close to zero.
questions.explanation: In reverse bias a diode has a very high resistance, so only a tiny current flows.

distractorRationales.text: A larger current than in forward bias.
distractorRationales.misconception: reverse bias confused with forward bias

distractorRationales.text: The same current as a fixed resistor.
A current that increases linearly with negative potential difference.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret reverse-bias behaviour for a diode.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Use data to decide whether a component is ohmic.

questions.stem: A lamp has a current of 0.20 A at 2.0 V and 0.32 A at 4.0 V. What does this show?
questions.question: A lamp has a current of 0.20 A at 2.0 V and 0.32 A at 4.0 V. What does this show?
options: The lamp is not ohmic because current has not doubled.
The lamp is ohmic because potential difference has doubled.
The lamp has zero resistance at 4.0 V.
The lamp transfers no energy at 2.0 V.
questions.correctAnswer: The lamp is not ohmic because current has not doubled.
questions.explanation: If the lamp were ohmic, doubling the potential difference would double the current. The current rises by less, so resistance has increased.

distractorRationales.text: The lamp is ohmic because potential difference has doubled.
The lamp has zero resistance at 4.0 V.
distractorRationales.misconception: non-linear graph confused with no resistance

distractorRationales.text: The lamp transfers no energy at 2.0 V.
distractorRationales.misconception: current flow confused with no energy transfer

questions.senNote: Checks: Use data to decide whether a component is ohmic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Choose meter connections for an I-V practical.

questions.stem: In an I-V investigation, how should the meters be connected to test a resistor?
questions.question: In an I-V investigation, how should the meters be connected to test a resistor?
options: Ammeter in series; voltmeter in parallel with the resistor.
Ammeter in parallel; voltmeter in series.
Both meters in series with the resistor.
Both meters in parallel with the resistor.
questions.correctAnswer: Ammeter in series; voltmeter in parallel with the resistor.
questions.explanation: Current is measured with an ammeter in series. Potential difference is measured with a voltmeter in parallel.

distractorRationales.text: Ammeter in parallel; voltmeter in series.
Both meters in series with the resistor.
Both meters in parallel with the resistor.

questions.senNote: Checks: Choose meter connections for an I-V practical.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the purpose of a variable resistor in an I-V practical.

questions.stem: Why is a variable resistor used in an I-V characteristics experiment?
questions.question: Why is a variable resistor used in an I-V characteristics experiment?
options: To change the current and potential difference readings.
To keep the potential difference fixed for every reading.
To stop charge flowing through the circuit.
questions.correctAnswer: To change the current and potential difference readings.
questions.explanation: A variable resistor allows different current and potential difference readings to be taken for the component being tested.

distractorRationales.text: To keep the potential difference fixed for every reading.
To stop charge flowing through the circuit.
distractorRationales.misconception: open switch confused with variable resistor

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the purpose of a variable resistor in an I-V practical.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why repeat readings improve an I-V investigation.

questions.stem: A student repeats each current reading three times in an I-V investigation. Why is this useful?
questions.question: A student repeats each current reading three times in an I-V investigation. Why is this useful?
options: It helps identify anomalous readings and calculate a mean.
It changes the resistance of an ohmic conductor to zero.
It removes the need for a voltmeter.
questions.correctAnswer: It helps identify anomalous readings and calculate a mean.
questions.explanation: Repeats make the data more reliable because anomalies can be spotted and a mean can be calculated.

distractorRationales.text: It changes the resistance of an ohmic conductor to zero.
distractorRationales.misconception: repeat readings confused with component change

distractorRationales.text: It removes the need for a voltmeter.

distractorRationales.misconception: practical method confused with component behaviour

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why repeat readings improve an I-V investigation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Compare resistance from I-V graph data.

questions.stem: Two straight I-V graphs pass through the origin. At 4 V, component X has a larger current than component Y. Which component has the lower resistance?
questions.question: Two straight I-V graphs pass through the origin. At 4 V, component X has a larger current than component Y. Which component has the lower resistance?
options: They must have the same resistance.
questions.explanation: For the same potential difference, the component with the larger current has the lower resistance.

distractorRationales.misconception: larger current interpreted as larger resistance

distractorRationales.text: They must have the same resistance.

distractorRationales.misconception: straight line confused with zero resistance

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare resistance from I-V graph data.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate resistance from one point on an I-V graph.

questions.stem: A component has a current of 0.25 A when the potential difference is 5.0 V. What is its resistance?
questions.question: A component has a current of 0.25 A when the potential difference is 5.0 V. What is its resistance?
questions.explanation: Use R = V ÷ I. R = 5.0 ÷ 0.25 = 20 ohms.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate resistance from one point on an I-V graph.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why temperature is controlled in an ohmic conductor investigation.

questions.stem: Why is constant temperature important when testing whether a conductor is ohmic?
questions.question: Why is constant temperature important when testing whether a conductor is ohmic?
options: The resistance of a metal conductor can change when its temperature changes.
Temperature changes the unit of current.
Temperature makes potential difference equal to charge.
A conductor cannot have current at room temperature.
questions.correctAnswer: The resistance of a metal conductor can change when its temperature changes.
questions.explanation: Ohm’s law applies to an ohmic conductor only when temperature is constant. Heating can change resistance.

distractorRationales.text: Temperature changes the unit of current.
Temperature makes potential difference equal to charge.
A conductor cannot have current at room temperature.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why temperature is controlled in an ohmic conductor investigation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Describe how filament lamp resistance changes with temperature.

questions.stem: A filament lamp is connected to a larger potential difference. What happens to the resistance of the filament?
questions.question: A filament lamp is connected to a larger potential difference. What happens to the resistance of the filament?
options: It increases because the filament gets hotter.
It decreases to zero because the lamp is brighter.
It stays exactly the same at all currents.
It becomes negative because current reverses.
questions.correctAnswer: It increases because the filament gets hotter.
questions.explanation: A larger potential difference gives a larger current, heating the filament. Its resistance increases.

distractorRationales.text: It decreases to zero because the lamp is brighter.
It stays exactly the same at all currents.
It becomes negative because current reverses.
distractorRationales.misconception: negative voltage confused with resistance sign

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe how filament lamp resistance changes with temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Recognise the I-V graph feature of a reverse-biased diode.

questions.stem: Which feature would you expect on the I-V graph for a diode?
questions.question: Which feature would you expect on the I-V graph for a diode?
options: Almost no current for reverse potential differences.
A straight line through the origin in both directions.
The same curve as a filament lamp.
A larger reverse current than forward current.
questions.correctAnswer: Almost no current for reverse potential differences.
questions.explanation: A diode has very high resistance in the reverse direction, so the current is almost zero there.

distractorRationales.text: A straight line through the origin in both directions.
The same curve as a filament lamp.
A larger reverse current than forward current.

questions.senNote: Checks: Recognise the I-V graph feature of a reverse-biased diode.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Identify a component that follows Ohm’s law.

questions.stem: A component has the same resistance for a range of potential differences, provided its temperature is constant. Which component is this most likely to be?
questions.question: A component has the same resistance for a range of potential differences, provided its temperature is constant. Which component is this most likely to be?
options: A diode in reverse bias only.
questions.explanation: A fixed resistor behaves as an ohmic conductor when its temperature is constant.

distractorRationales.misconception: lamp resistance changes as it heats

distractorRationales.text: A diode in reverse bias only.

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify a component that follows Ohm’s law.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Respond appropriately to an anomalous I-V reading.

questions.stem: In an I-V practical, one current reading is much higher than the pattern of the other readings. What should the student do first?
questions.question: In an I-V practical, one current reading is much higher than the pattern of the other readings. What should the student do first?
options: Repeat the reading to check whether it is anomalous.
Change the unit from amperes to volts.
Assume the component is a diode.
questions.correctAnswer: Repeat the reading to check whether it is anomalous.
questions.explanation: A suspect reading should be repeated before deciding whether it is anomalous.

distractorRationales.text: Change the unit from amperes to volts.
distractorRationales.misconception: unit change does not fix data quality

distractorRationales.text: Assume the component is a diode.

questions.senNote: Checks: Respond appropriately to an anomalous I-V reading.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Explain a method choice in a filament lamp I-V practical.

questions.stem: Why should the switch be opened between readings when testing a filament lamp?
questions.question: Why should the switch be opened between readings when testing a filament lamp?
options: To reduce heating of the filament between readings.
To make the ammeter read potential difference.
To reverse the direction of conventional current.
To make the lamp an ohmic conductor.
questions.correctAnswer: To reduce heating of the filament between readings.
questions.explanation: Opening the switch reduces heating, which helps limit changes in resistance during the investigation.

distractorRationales.text: To make the ammeter read potential difference.
To reverse the direction of conventional current.
To make the lamp an ohmic conductor.
distractorRationales.misconception: cooling does not make all lamp data ohmic

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain a method choice in a filament lamp I-V practical.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Investigate and interpret current-potential difference graphs.

learningObjective.statement: Predict current for an ohmic conductor using proportionality.

questions.stem: A resistor has a current of 0.10 A at 2.0 V and behaves ohmically. What current is expected at 6.0 V?
questions.question: A resistor has a current of 0.10 A at 2.0 V and behaves ohmically. What current is expected at 6.0 V?
questions.explanation: For an ohmic conductor, current is directly proportional to potential difference. Tripling 2.0 V to 6.0 V triples 0.10 A to 0.30 A.
questions.senNote: Checks: Predict current for an ohmic conductor using proportionality.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the live wire as the dangerous high-potential wire.

questions.stem: Which wire in a mains cable is at a high potential difference compared with earth?
questions.question: Which wire in a mains cable is at a high potential difference compared with earth?
questions.explanation: The live wire is at about 230 V compared with earth and can be dangerous.
questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the live wire as the dangerous high-potential wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the function of the neutral wire.

questions.stem: What is the function of the neutral wire in a domestic mains circuit?
questions.question: What is the function of the neutral wire in a domestic mains circuit?
options: It completes the circuit and is close to earth potential.
It is the safety wire connected to the metal case.
It is always at 230 V compared with earth.
questions.correctAnswer: It completes the circuit and is close to earth potential.
questions.explanation: The neutral wire completes the circuit and is normally close to 0 V compared with earth.

distractorRationales.text: It is the safety wire connected to the metal case.

distractorRationales.misconception: fuse position confused with neutral wire

distractorRationales.text: It is always at 230 V compared with earth.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the function of the neutral wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how the earth wire helps protect users.

questions.stem: A metal-cased appliance has an earth wire. What is the purpose of the earth wire?
questions.question: A metal-cased appliance has an earth wire. What is the purpose of the earth wire?
options: It provides a low-resistance path to earth if the case becomes live.
It makes the appliance use less energy.
It makes the neutral wire 230 V.
questions.correctAnswer: It provides a low-resistance path to earth if the case becomes live.
questions.explanation: If a fault makes the metal case live, the earth wire carries a large current to earth so the safety device can disconnect the supply.

distractorRationales.text: It makes the appliance use less energy.

distractorRationales.misconception: earth wire confused with supply wire

distractorRationales.text: It makes the neutral wire 230 V.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how the earth wire helps protect users.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why a fuse is placed in the live wire.

questions.stem: Why should a fuse be connected in the live wire of a plug?
questions.question: Why should a fuse be connected in the live wire of a plug?
options: So melting the fuse disconnects the appliance from the live supply.
So the neutral wire becomes live.
So the earth wire carries current all the time.
So the appliance has a larger power rating.
questions.correctAnswer: So melting the fuse disconnects the appliance from the live supply.
questions.explanation: A fuse in the live wire breaks the live connection when the current is too large, making the appliance safer.

distractorRationales.text: So the neutral wire becomes live.
So the earth wire carries current all the time.
distractorRationales.misconception: earth wire normally carries no current

distractorRationales.text: So the appliance has a larger power rating.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why a fuse is placed in the live wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why switches are fitted in the live wire.

questions.stem: Why should a switch in a mains circuit be connected in the live wire?
questions.question: Why should a switch in a mains circuit be connected in the live wire?
options: Opening the switch disconnects the appliance from the live supply.
Opening the switch makes the earth wire live.
The switch must carry current through the neutral wire only.
A switch in the live wire increases the mains voltage.
questions.correctAnswer: Opening the switch disconnects the appliance from the live supply.
questions.explanation: A switch in the live wire disconnects the appliance from the dangerous high-potential wire when it is off.

distractorRationales.text: Opening the switch makes the earth wire live.
The switch must carry current through the neutral wire only.
A switch in the live wire increases the mains voltage.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why switches are fitted in the live wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Choose a suitable fuse rating for an appliance.

questions.stem: An appliance normally takes a current of 2.2 A. Which fuse rating is most suitable?
questions.question: An appliance normally takes a current of 2.2 A. Which fuse rating is most suitable?
questions.explanation: The fuse rating should be just above the normal current, so 3 A is the most suitable choice.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose a suitable fuse rating for an appliance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Describe what happens during a live-to-case fault in an earthed appliance.

questions.stem: A fault causes the live wire to touch the metal case of an earthed appliance. What happens next?
questions.question: A fault causes the live wire to touch the metal case of an earthed appliance. What happens next?
options: A large current flows through the earth wire and the fuse melts.
The case stays live until someone touches it.
The appliance becomes double insulated automatically.
questions.correctAnswer: A large current flows through the earth wire and the fuse melts.
questions.explanation: The earth wire provides a low-resistance path, causing a large current that melts the fuse and disconnects the live supply.

distractorRationales.text: The case stays live until someone touches it.

distractorRationales.misconception: wire colour confused with fault response

distractorRationales.text: The appliance becomes double insulated automatically.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe what happens during a live-to-case fault in an earthed appliance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why double-insulated appliances do not need an earth wire.

questions.stem: Why does a double-insulated appliance not need an earth wire?
questions.question: Why does a double-insulated appliance not need an earth wire?
options: It has an insulating case, so the user cannot touch a live metal case.
It uses direct current instead of mains electricity.
It has a neutral wire at 230 V.
questions.correctAnswer: It has an insulating case, so the user cannot touch a live metal case.
questions.explanation: A double-insulated appliance has extra insulation and no exposed metal case that could become live.

distractorRationales.misconception: double insulated appliances still use live supply

distractorRationales.text: It uses direct current instead of mains electricity.
It has a neutral wire at 230 V.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why double-insulated appliances do not need an earth wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the approximate UK mains voltage and frequency.

questions.stem: What are the approximate voltage and frequency of the UK mains supply?
questions.question: What are the approximate voltage and frequency of the UK mains supply?
questions.explanation: The UK mains supply is alternating current at about 230 V and 50 Hz.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the approximate UK mains voltage and frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Describe alternating current in the domestic mains supply.

questions.stem: What does it mean when the mains supply is described as alternating current?
questions.question: What does it mean when the mains supply is described as alternating current?
options: The current always flows in one direction.
The current is stored in the plug.
questions.explanation: In an alternating current supply, the current changes direction repeatedly.

distractorRationales.text: The current always flows in one direction.
distractorRationales.misconception: direct current confused with alternating current

distractorRationales.text: The current is stored in the plug.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe alternating current in the domestic mains supply.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Explain the danger of a live-to-earth connection through a person.

questions.stem: Why is it dangerous for a person to touch the live wire while also connected to earth?
questions.question: Why is it dangerous for a person to touch the live wire while also connected to earth?
options: A current can pass through the person to earth.
The person becomes electrically neutral, so no current flows.
The live wire changes into a neutral wire.
questions.correctAnswer: A current can pass through the person to earth.
questions.explanation: Touching live while connected to earth can complete a path for current through the body.

distractorRationales.text: The person becomes electrically neutral, so no current flows.
The live wire changes into a neutral wire.

distractorRationales.misconception: fuse rating confused with shock risk

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain the danger of a live-to-earth connection through a person.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why switching the neutral wire does not make an appliance fully safe.

questions.stem: An appliance is switched off, but the switch is in the neutral wire. Why can this still be dangerous?
questions.question: An appliance is switched off, but the switch is in the neutral wire. Why can this still be dangerous?
options: Parts of the appliance may still be connected to the live wire.
The live wire becomes harmless whenever any switch is open.
The earth wire becomes the power supply.
The fuse cannot melt in any circuit.
questions.correctAnswer: Parts of the appliance may still be connected to the live wire.
questions.explanation: If only the neutral wire is disconnected, parts of the appliance can remain connected to the live supply.

distractorRationales.text: The live wire becomes harmless whenever any switch is open.
The earth wire becomes the power supply.
The fuse cannot melt in any circuit.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why switching the neutral wire does not make an appliance fully safe.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the colour of the earth wire.

questions.stem: Which colour identifies the earth wire in a modern UK plug?
questions.question: Which colour identifies the earth wire in a modern UK plug?
questions.explanation: In modern UK wiring, the earth wire is green and yellow. The live wire is brown and the neutral wire is blue.
questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the colour of the earth wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the colour of the live wire.

questions.stem: Which wire in a modern UK plug is brown?
questions.question: Which wire in a modern UK plug is brown?
questions.explanation: The live wire is brown. The neutral wire is blue and the earth wire is green and yellow.
questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the colour of the live wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Analyse the risk when the earth wire is missing during a live-case fault.

questions.stem: A metal kettle has a damaged live wire touching its case, but the earth wire is broken. What is the main danger?
questions.question: A metal kettle has a damaged live wire touching its case, but the earth wire is broken. What is the main danger?
options: The metal case can remain live and give an electric shock.
The case becomes neutral and safe to touch.
The fuse must melt instantly without an earth path.
The kettle changes from a.c. to d.c.
questions.correctAnswer: The metal case can remain live and give an electric shock.
questions.explanation: Without a working earth wire, the metal case may stay live because there may not be a large enough current to melt the fuse.

distractorRationales.text: The case becomes neutral and safe to touch.
The fuse must melt instantly without an earth path.
distractorRationales.misconception: earth path needed for large fault current

distractorRationales.text: The kettle changes from a.c. to d.c.

questions.senNote: Checks: Analyse the risk when the earth wire is missing during a live-case fault.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the safety function of a circuit breaker.

questions.stem: What is the safety role of a circuit breaker?
questions.question: What is the safety role of a circuit breaker?
options: It disconnects the circuit when the current becomes too large.
It increases the current when an appliance starts.
It stores electrical energy for later use.
It changes a.c. into d.c. for every appliance.
questions.correctAnswer: It disconnects the circuit when the current becomes too large.
questions.explanation: A circuit breaker opens the circuit when the current is too high, reducing the risk of overheating and shock.

distractorRationales.text: It increases the current when an appliance starts.
It stores electrical energy for later use.
It changes a.c. into d.c. for every appliance.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the safety function of a circuit breaker.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Relate appliance power rating to rate of energy transfer.

questions.stem: Two kettles are used for the same time. Kettle X has a power rating of 3.0 kW and kettle Y has a power rating of 1.5 kW. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: Two kettles are used for the same time. Kettle X has a power rating of 3.0 kW and kettle Y has a power rating of 1.5 kW. Which statement is correct?
options: Kettle X transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle Y.
Kettle Y transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle X.
Both kettles transfer energy at the same rate.
Power rating tells you the mass of the kettle.
questions.correctAnswer: Kettle X transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle Y.
questions.explanation: Power is the rate of energy transfer. 3.0 kW is twice 1.5 kW, so kettle X transfers energy twice as quickly.

distractorRationales.text: Kettle Y transfers energy at twice the rate of kettle X.
Both kettles transfer energy at the same rate.
Power rating tells you the mass of the kettle.

questions.senNote: Checks: Relate appliance power rating to rate of energy transfer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate operating current from power and mains voltage.

questions.stem: A 690 W appliance is connected to the 230 V mains. What current does it normally take?
questions.question: A 690 W appliance is connected to the 230 V mains. What current does it normally take?
questions.explanation: Use P = V × I, so I = P ÷ V. I = 690 ÷ 230 = 3.0 A.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate operating current from power and mains voltage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Explain mains electricity safety including fuses and earthing.

learningObjective.statement: Apply domestic electricity safety rules to damaged insulation.

questions.stem: A plug has cracked insulation so that bare copper from the live wire can be touched. What should be done?
questions.question: A plug has cracked insulation so that bare copper from the live wire can be touched. What should be done?
options: Stop using the appliance until the plug or cable is repaired.
Use the appliance only when the switch is off.
Replace the earth wire with a thicker live wire.
Wrap the fuse in foil so it cannot melt.
questions.correctAnswer: Stop using the appliance until the plug or cable is repaired.
questions.explanation: Damaged insulation can expose the live conductor and cause electric shock, so the appliance should not be used until repaired.

distractorRationales.text: Use the appliance only when the switch is off.
distractorRationales.misconception: live wire may still be dangerous

distractorRationales.text: Replace the earth wire with a thicker live wire.
Wrap the fuse in foil so it cannot melt.

questions.senNote: Checks: Apply domestic electricity safety rules to damaged insulation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Describe an electrical energy transfer in an appliance.

questions.stem: An electric heater is switched on. Which statement describes the useful energy transfer?
questions.question: An electric heater is switched on. Which statement describes the useful energy transfer?
options: Energy is transferred electrically to the thermal store of the room.
Charge is destroyed inside the heater.
Potential difference is measured in joules.
Current is stored in the heating element.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is transferred electrically to the thermal store of the room.
questions.explanation: A heater transfers energy electrically. The useful output is an increase in thermal energy of the surroundings.

distractorRationales.text: Charge is destroyed inside the heater.
Potential difference is measured in joules.
Current is stored in the heating element.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe an electrical energy transfer in an appliance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the unit of energy transferred.

questions.stem: Which unit is used for energy transferred?
questions.question: Which unit is used for energy transferred?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred is measured in joules. Amperes measure current and volts measure potential difference.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the unit of energy transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred using power and time.

questions.stem: A 40 W fan is used for 300 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.question: A 40 W fan is used for 300 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred = power x time, so E = 40 x 300 = 12000 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred using power and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate time from energy transferred and power.

questions.stem: A lamp transfers 900 J of energy at a power of 15 W. For how long is it switched on?
questions.question: A lamp transfers 900 J of energy at a power of 15 W. For how long is it switched on?
questions.explanation: Time = energy transferred / power, so t = 900 / 15 = 60 s.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate time from energy transferred and power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate power from energy transferred and time.

questions.stem: A small motor transfers 2400 J of energy in 80 s. What is its power?
questions.question: A small motor transfers 2400 J of energy in 80 s. What is its power?
questions.explanation: Power = energy transferred / time, so P = 2400 / 80 = 30 W.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate power from energy transferred and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred using charge and potential difference.

questions.stem: A charge of 5 C passes through a component with a potential difference of 12 V. How much energy is transferred?
questions.question: A charge of 5 C passes through a component with a potential difference of 12 V. How much energy is transferred?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred = charge x potential difference, so E = 5 x 12 = 60 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred using charge and potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate charge from energy transferred and potential difference.

questions.stem: A component transfers 72 J of energy when connected across 9 V. How much charge passes through it?
questions.question: A component transfers 72 J of energy when connected across 9 V. How much charge passes through it?
questions.explanation: Charge = energy transferred / potential difference, so Q = 72 / 9 = 8 C.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate charge from energy transferred and potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate potential difference using energy transferred and charge.

questions.stem: A charge of 4 C transfers 48 J of energy in a component. What is the potential difference across the component?
questions.question: A charge of 4 C transfers 48 J of energy in a component. What is the potential difference across the component?
questions.explanation: Potential difference = energy transferred / charge, so V = 48 / 4 = 12 V.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate potential difference using energy transferred and charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred in kilowatt-hours.

questions.stem: A 0.8 kW microwave is used for 0.25 hours. How much energy does it transfer?
questions.question: A 0.8 kW microwave is used for 0.25 hours. How much energy does it transfer?
questions.explanation: Energy in kWh = power in kW x time in hours, so 0.8 x 0.25 = 0.20 kWh.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred in kilowatt-hours.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate the cost of electrical energy use.

questions.stem: A washing machine uses 1.8 kWh. Electricity costs 30 p per kWh. What is the cost of using the washing machine?
questions.question: A washing machine uses 1.8 kWh. Electricity costs 30 p per kWh. What is the cost of using the washing machine?
questions.explanation: Cost = energy used x cost per kWh, so 1.8 x 30 p = 54 p.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate the cost of electrical energy use.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Compare energy transferred using power ratings.

questions.stem: A 1 kW heater and a 2 kW heater are each used for 30 minutes. Which heater transfers more energy?
questions.question: A 1 kW heater and a 2 kW heater are each used for 30 minutes. Which heater transfers more energy?
options: The 1 kW heater, because it has the lower power.
The 2 kW heater, because it transfers energy at a greater rate.
Both heaters transfer the same energy because the time is the same.
Neither heater transfers energy unless the time is measured in seconds.
questions.correctAnswer: The 2 kW heater, because it transfers energy at a greater rate.
questions.explanation: For the same time, the appliance with the greater power transfers more energy.

distractorRationales.text: The 1 kW heater, because it has the lower power.
Both heaters transfer the same energy because the time is the same.
Neither heater transfers energy unless the time is measured in seconds.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare energy transferred using power ratings.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Explain wasted energy in an electrical device.

questions.stem: A phone charger transfers 18000 J from the mains. The phone battery gains 12600 J. What has happened to the remaining energy?
questions.question: A phone charger transfers 18000 J from the mains. The phone battery gains 12600 J. What has happened to the remaining energy?
options: It has been wasted, mostly by heating the charger and surroundings.
It has disappeared from the circuit.
It has turned into extra charge.
questions.correctAnswer: It has been wasted, mostly by heating the charger and surroundings.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved. The remaining energy is transferred to less useful stores, mainly heating the charger and surroundings.

distractorRationales.text: It has disappeared from the circuit.
It has turned into extra charge.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain wasted energy in an electrical device.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how power affects energy cost over time.

questions.stem: Why does a lower-power lamp usually cost less to run for the same number of hours?
questions.question: Why does a lower-power lamp usually cost less to run for the same number of hours?
options: It transfers less energy in the same time.
It has no current through it.
It does not need a complete circuit.
It makes the electricity meter run backwards.
questions.correctAnswer: It transfers less energy in the same time.
questions.explanation: For the same time, a lower power means less energy transferred, so the cost is lower.

distractorRationales.text: It has no current through it.
It does not need a complete circuit.
It makes the electricity meter run backwards.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how power affects energy cost over time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret the meaning of a kilowatt-hour.

questions.stem: Electricity bills often measure energy in kilowatt-hours. What does one kilowatt-hour mean?
questions.question: Electricity bills often measure energy in kilowatt-hours. What does one kilowatt-hour mean?
options: The energy transferred by a 1 kW appliance used for 1 hour.
The current in a 1 kW appliance after 1 hour.
The potential difference supplied by the mains for 1 hour.
The resistance of a 1 kW appliance.
questions.correctAnswer: The energy transferred by a 1 kW appliance used for 1 hour.
questions.explanation: One kilowatt-hour is the energy transferred by a 1 kW appliance operating for 1 hour.

distractorRationales.text: The current in a 1 kW appliance after 1 hour.
The potential difference supplied by the mains for 1 hour.
The resistance of a 1 kW appliance.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret the meaning of a kilowatt-hour.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Convert one kilowatt-hour into joules.

questions.stem: Which energy transfer is equal to 1 kWh?
questions.question: Which energy transfer is equal to 1 kWh?
questions.senNote: Checks: Convert one kilowatt-hour into joules.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Choose the correct time unit for kWh calculations.

questions.stem: A toaster is labelled 1.2 kW and is used for 5 minutes. Which calculation gives the energy transferred in kWh?
questions.question: A toaster is labelled 1.2 kW and is used for 5 minutes. Which calculation gives the energy transferred in kWh?
questions.explanation: For kWh, power must be in kW and time in hours. Five minutes is 5 / 60 hours.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose the correct time unit for kWh calculations.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Relate potential difference to energy transferred per unit charge.

questions.stem: A battery transfers energy to charges in a circuit. Which quantity tells you the energy transferred per coulomb of charge?
questions.question: A battery transfers energy to charges in a circuit. Which quantity tells you the energy transferred per coulomb of charge?
questions.explanation: Potential difference is energy transferred per coulomb of charge.
questions.senNote: Checks: Relate potential difference to energy transferred per unit charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use electrical energy transfer equations.

learningObjective.statement: Apply conservation of energy to an electrical appliance.

questions.stem: An electrical appliance is not 100% efficient. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: An electrical appliance is not 100% efficient. Which statement is correct?
options: Some energy is transferred to less useful stores.
Some energy is destroyed inside the appliance.
The appliance has no input energy.
The useful output energy is always larger than the input energy.
questions.correctAnswer: Some energy is transferred to less useful stores.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved, but not all transferred energy is useful. Some is dissipated to less useful stores.

distractorRationales.text: Some energy is destroyed inside the appliance.
The appliance has no input energy.
The useful output energy is always larger than the input energy.

questions.senNote: Checks: Apply conservation of energy to an electrical appliance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: State power as the rate of energy transfer.

questions.stem: What does the power rating of an electrical appliance tell you?
questions.question: What does the power rating of an electrical appliance tell you?
options: The rate at which it transfers energy.
The total charge stored inside it.
The number of electrons in each wire.
questions.correctAnswer: The rate at which it transfers energy.
questions.explanation: Power is the rate of energy transfer. A higher power rating means energy is transferred more quickly.

distractorRationales.text: The total charge stored inside it.
The number of electrons in each wire.

questions.senNote: Checks: State power as the rate of energy transfer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the unit of electrical power.

questions.stem: Which unit is used for electrical power?
questions.question: Which unit is used for electrical power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in watts. One watt is one joule per second.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the unit of electrical power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate power using potential difference and current.

questions.stem: A motor has a potential difference of 12 V across it and a current of 3 A through it. What is its power?
questions.question: A motor has a potential difference of 12 V across it and a current of 3 A through it. What is its power?
questions.explanation: Power = potential difference x current, so P = 12 x 3 = 36 W.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate power using potential difference and current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate current from power and potential difference.

questions.stem: A 60 W lamp is connected to a 12 V supply. What current flows through the lamp?
questions.question: A 60 W lamp is connected to a 12 V supply. What current flows through the lamp?
questions.explanation: Current = power / potential difference, so I = 60 / 12 = 5 A.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate current from power and potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate potential difference from power and current.

questions.stem: A heater transfers energy at 920 W when the current is 4 A. What is the potential difference across the heater?
questions.question: A heater transfers energy at 920 W when the current is 4 A. What is the potential difference across the heater?
questions.explanation: Potential difference = power / current, so V = 920 / 4 = 230 V.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate potential difference from power and current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate power using current and resistance.

questions.stem: A resistor has a resistance of 5 ohms and a current of 2 A. What is the power transferred by the resistor?
questions.question: A resistor has a resistance of 5 ohms and a current of 2 A. What is the power transferred by the resistor?
questions.explanation: Power = current squared x resistance, so P = 2^2 x 5 = 20 W.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate power using current and resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Rearrange the power equation involving resistance.

questions.stem: A component transfers 45 W of power when the current is 3 A. What is its resistance?
questions.question: A component transfers 45 W of power when the current is 3 A. What is its resistance?
questions.explanation: Using P = I^2 R, resistance = P / I^2 = 45 / 9 = 5 ohms.
questions.senNote: Checks: Rearrange the power equation involving resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate power from energy transferred and time.

questions.stem: A device transfers 1800 J of energy in 30 s. What is its power?
questions.question: A device transfers 1800 J of energy in 30 s. What is its power?
questions.explanation: Power = energy transferred / time, so P = 1800 / 30 = 60 W.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate power from energy transferred and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Convert kilowatts to watts.

questions.stem: An electric kettle has a power rating of 2.4 kW. What is this power in watts?
questions.question: An electric kettle has a power rating of 2.4 kW. What is this power in watts?
questions.senNote: Checks: Convert kilowatts to watts.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Compare appliances using power ratings.

questions.stem: Two lamps are connected to the same potential difference. Lamp A is rated at 20 W and lamp B is rated at 60 W. Which lamp transfers energy faster?
questions.question: Two lamps are connected to the same potential difference. Lamp A is rated at 20 W and lamp B is rated at 60 W. Which lamp transfers energy faster?
options: Lamp A, because it has the smaller rating.
Lamp B, because it has the larger power rating.
Both lamps transfer energy at the same rate.
Neither lamp transfers energy unless the current is zero.
questions.correctAnswer: Lamp B, because it has the larger power rating.
questions.explanation: The lamp with the larger power rating transfers energy at a greater rate.

distractorRationales.text: Lamp A, because it has the smaller rating.
Both lamps transfer energy at the same rate.
Neither lamp transfers energy unless the current is zero.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare appliances using power ratings.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Choose a fuse rating using power and potential difference.

questions.stem: A 690 W appliance is used on a 230 V supply. Which fuse rating is most suitable?
questions.question: A 690 W appliance is used on a 230 V supply. Which fuse rating is most suitable?
questions.explanation: The current is I = P / V = 690 / 230 = 3 A. A 3 A fuse is suitable.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose a fuse rating using power and potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred in kilowatt-hours.

questions.stem: A 1.5 kW heater is used for 2 hours. How much energy is transferred in kilowatt-hours?
questions.question: A 1.5 kW heater is used for 2 hours. How much energy is transferred in kilowatt-hours?
questions.explanation: Energy in kWh = power in kW x time in hours, so 1.5 x 2 = 3.0 kWh.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred in kilowatt-hours.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate the cost of electrical energy.

questions.stem: Electricity costs 28 p per kWh. A heater uses 3 kWh. What is the cost?
questions.question: Electricity costs 28 p per kWh. A heater uses 3 kWh. What is the cost?
questions.explanation: Cost = energy used x cost per kWh, so 3 x 28 p = 84 p.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate the cost of electrical energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why lower-power appliances use less energy for the same time.

questions.stem: Why does replacing a 60 W filament lamp with a 9 W LED reduce energy use for the same time switched on?
questions.question: Why does replacing a 60 W filament lamp with a 9 W LED reduce energy use for the same time switched on?
options: The LED transfers energy at a lower rate.
The LED has no current through it.
The filament lamp has zero resistance.
The LED makes electricity instead of using it.
questions.correctAnswer: The LED transfers energy at a lower rate.
questions.explanation: A lower power rating means less energy is transferred each second, so less energy is used over the same time.

distractorRationales.text: The LED has no current through it.
The filament lamp has zero resistance.
The LED makes electricity instead of using it.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why lower-power appliances use less energy for the same time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate maximum power from supply limits.

questions.stem: A power supply can safely provide a maximum current of 2 A at 12 V. What is the maximum power it can provide?
questions.question: A power supply can safely provide a maximum current of 2 A at 12 V. What is the maximum power it can provide?
questions.explanation: Power = potential difference x current, so P = 12 x 2 = 24 W.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate maximum power from supply limits.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Use a power-time graph to calculate energy transferred.

questions.stem: A power-time graph is horizontal at 40 W for 5 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.question: A power-time graph is horizontal at 40 W for 5 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred is the area under a power-time graph. E = 40 x 5 = 200 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Use a power-time graph to calculate energy transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the heating effect of a current in a resistor.

questions.stem: A resistor gets warmer when a current passes through it. What energy transfer is taking place?
questions.question: A resistor gets warmer when a current passes through it. What energy transfer is taking place?
options: Electrical energy is transferred to the thermal store of the resistor.
Thermal energy is transferred to the electrical store of the battery.
Nuclear energy is transferred to the chemical store of the resistor.
Kinetic energy is transferred to gravitational potential energy only.
questions.correctAnswer: Electrical energy is transferred to the thermal store of the resistor.
questions.explanation: Current through a resistor transfers electrical energy to the thermal store of the resistor and its surroundings.

distractorRationales.text: Thermal energy is transferred to the electrical store of the battery.
Nuclear energy is transferred to the chemical store of the resistor.
Kinetic energy is transferred to gravitational potential energy only.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the heating effect of a current in a resistor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Compare energy transfer using power and time.

questions.stem: A 100 W device and a 200 W device are each used for 10 s. Which device transfers more energy?
questions.question: A 100 W device and a 200 W device are each used for 10 s. Which device transfers more energy?
options: The 100 W device, because it has the lower power.
The 200 W device, because it transfers energy twice as fast.
Both transfer the same energy because they are used for the same time.
Neither transfers energy unless the time is measured in hours.
questions.correctAnswer: The 200 W device, because it transfers energy twice as fast.
questions.explanation: For the same time, the device with twice the power transfers twice as much energy.

distractorRationales.text: The 100 W device, because it has the lower power.
Both transfer the same energy because they are used for the same time.
Neither transfers energy unless the time is measured in hours.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare energy transfer using power and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use power equations in electrical circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Explain an overload risk using current and power.

questions.stem: Why can using too many high-power appliances on one extension lead be dangerous?
questions.question: Why can using too many high-power appliances on one extension lead be dangerous?
options: The total current can become too large and overheat the lead.
The mains potential difference falls to zero instantly.
The lead becomes a perfect insulator.
questions.correctAnswer: The total current can become too large and overheat the lead.
questions.explanation: High-power appliances can draw large currents. If the total current is too large, wires can overheat.

distractorRationales.text: The mains potential difference falls to zero instantly.
The lead becomes a perfect insulator.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain an overload risk using current and power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the structure of a parallel circuit.

questions.stem: Which circuit has components connected in parallel?
questions.question: Which circuit has components connected in parallel?
options: A circuit with more than one path for current.
A circuit with only one path for current.
A circuit with no source of potential difference.
A circuit where current cannot split at any point.
questions.correctAnswer: A circuit with more than one path for current.
questions.explanation: A parallel circuit has branches, giving current more than one path to follow.

distractorRationales.text: A circuit with only one path for current.
A circuit with no source of potential difference.
A circuit where current cannot split at any point.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the structure of a parallel circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Recall that potential difference is the same across parallel branches.

questions.stem: Two lamps are connected in parallel to a 6 V battery. What is the potential difference across each lamp?
questions.question: Two lamps are connected in parallel to a 6 V battery. What is the potential difference across each lamp?
questions.explanation: Components in parallel are connected across the same two points, so each branch has the full supply potential difference.

distractorRationales.misconception: parallel potential difference confused with series sharing

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall that potential difference is the same across parallel branches.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Use conservation of current at a junction.

questions.stem: A current of 0.60 A reaches a junction. One branch has a current of 0.20 A. What is the current in the other branch?
questions.question: A current of 0.60 A reaches a junction. One branch has a current of 0.20 A. What is the current in the other branch?
questions.explanation: Current is conserved at a junction. The branch currents add to the total, so the other branch is 0.60 - 0.20 = 0.40 A.
questions.senNote: Checks: Use conservation of current at a junction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate total current in a parallel circuit.

questions.stem: A parallel circuit has branch currents of 0.30 A and 0.45 A. What is the total current from the cell?
questions.question: A parallel circuit has branch currents of 0.30 A and 0.45 A. What is the total current from the cell?
questions.explanation: The total current is the sum of the branch currents: 0.30 + 0.45 = 0.75 A.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate total current in a parallel circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Describe how adding a parallel resistor affects total resistance.

questions.stem: A second identical resistor is added in parallel with the first. What happens to the total resistance of the circuit?
questions.question: A second identical resistor is added in parallel with the first. What happens to the total resistance of the circuit?
questions.explanation: Adding a parallel branch gives charge another path, so the total resistance decreases.

distractorRationales.misconception: parallel resistors confused with series resistors
parallel path confused with broken circuit

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe how adding a parallel resistor affects total resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Predict the effect of a broken branch in a parallel circuit.

questions.stem: Two lamps are connected in parallel. One lamp breaks. What happens to the other lamp?
questions.question: Two lamps are connected in parallel. One lamp breaks. What happens to the other lamp?
options: It stays lit if its branch is still complete.
It must go out because all current used the broken lamp first.
It becomes twice as bright in every circuit.
It changes into a resistor with no current.
questions.correctAnswer: It stays lit if its branch is still complete.
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, each branch has its own path. A break in one branch does not necessarily break the other branch.

distractorRationales.text: It must go out because all current used the broken lamp first.
distractorRationales.misconception: series behaviour applied to parallel circuit

distractorRationales.text: It becomes twice as bright in every circuit.
It changes into a resistor with no current.

questions.senNote: Checks: Predict the effect of a broken branch in a parallel circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Compare the structure of series and parallel circuits.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly compares series and parallel circuits?
questions.question: Which statement correctly compares series and parallel circuits?
options: Series circuits have one path; parallel circuits have more than one path.
Series circuits always have a larger supply voltage.
questions.correctAnswer: Series circuits have one path; parallel circuits have more than one path.
questions.explanation: A series circuit has a single loop. A parallel circuit has branches and junctions.

distractorRationales.text: Series circuits always have a larger supply voltage.

distractorRationales.misconception: component type confused with circuit type

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare the structure of series and parallel circuits.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Relate branch resistance to branch current in parallel.

questions.stem: Two parallel branches are connected to the same battery. Branch X has a lower resistance than branch Y. Which branch has the larger current?
questions.question: Two parallel branches are connected to the same battery. Branch X has a lower resistance than branch Y. Which branch has the larger current?
options: Both branches must have zero current.
Both branches must have exactly the same current.
questions.explanation: The potential difference is the same across each branch. The branch with lower resistance has the larger current.

distractorRationales.misconception: higher resistance incorrectly linked to larger current

distractorRationales.text: Both branches must have zero current.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel circuit mistaken for open circuit

distractorRationales.text: Both branches must have exactly the same current.
distractorRationales.misconception: same potential difference confused with same current

questions.senNote: Checks: Relate branch resistance to branch current in parallel.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Choose where to connect an ammeter in a parallel branch.

questions.stem: Where should an ammeter be placed to measure the current in one branch of a parallel circuit?
questions.question: Where should an ammeter be placed to measure the current in one branch of a parallel circuit?
options: In series with the component in that branch.
In parallel across the whole battery.
Between two points without completing a path.
questions.correctAnswer: In series with the component in that branch.
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures the current through a branch, so it must be connected in series within that branch.

distractorRationales.text: In parallel across the whole battery.

distractorRationales.misconception: switch measurement confused with branch current

distractorRationales.text: Between two points without completing a path.
distractorRationales.misconception: ammeter must be part of circuit path

questions.senNote: Checks: Choose where to connect an ammeter in a parallel branch.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Choose where to connect a voltmeter in a parallel circuit.

questions.stem: How should a voltmeter be connected to measure the potential difference across one branch?
questions.question: How should a voltmeter be connected to measure the potential difference across one branch?
options: Only on the live side of the battery.
questions.explanation: A voltmeter compares two points, so it is connected in parallel across the branch or component.

distractorRationales.misconception: ammeter style connection confused with voltmeter

distractorRationales.text: Only on the live side of the battery.

distractorRationales.misconception: meter replacing component rather than measuring across it

questions.senNote: Checks: Choose where to connect a voltmeter in a parallel circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Identify evidence for equal potential difference across parallel branches.

questions.stem: In a practical, a student tests two resistors in parallel. Which measurement would show that the branches have the same potential difference?
questions.question: In a practical, a student tests two resistors in parallel. Which measurement would show that the branches have the same potential difference?
options: A voltmeter reading across each branch.
An ammeter reading before the first branch only.
A thermometer reading on each resistor.
A ruler measurement of the wire length.
questions.correctAnswer: A voltmeter reading across each branch.
questions.explanation: Potential difference is measured with a voltmeter connected across the two points being compared.

distractorRationales.text: An ammeter reading before the first branch only.
distractorRationales.misconception: current reading confused with potential difference

distractorRationales.text: A thermometer reading on each resistor.
A ruler measurement of the wire length.
distractorRationales.misconception: wire length not direct p.d. measurement

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify evidence for equal potential difference across parallel branches.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Apply current conservation to a three-branch parallel circuit.

questions.stem: A current of 1.2 A leaves a cell and splits into three branches. Two branch currents are 0.50 A and 0.30 A. What is the third branch current?
questions.question: A current of 1.2 A leaves a cell and splits into three branches. Two branch currents are 0.50 A and 0.30 A. What is the third branch current?
questions.explanation: The branch currents must add to 1.2 A. The missing current is 1.2 - 0.50 - 0.30 = 0.40 A.
questions.senNote: Checks: Apply current conservation to a three-branch parallel circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Predict total current when another parallel branch is added.

questions.stem: A third lamp is added in parallel to two lamps already connected to a battery. What happens to the total current from the battery?
questions.question: A third lamp is added in parallel to two lamps already connected to a battery. What happens to the total current from the battery?
options: It becomes the same as the current in one branch.
questions.explanation: Adding another parallel branch decreases total resistance, so the total current from the battery increases.

distractorRationales.misconception: extra branch confused with broken circuit

distractorRationales.text: It becomes the same as the current in one branch.

questions.senNote: Checks: Predict total current when another parallel branch is added.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Compare total parallel resistance with individual branch resistance.

questions.stem: Two 10 ohm resistors are connected in parallel. How does their total resistance compare with 10 ohms?
questions.question: Two 10 ohm resistors are connected in parallel. How does their total resistance compare with 10 ohms?
options: It is impossible for current to flow.
questions.explanation: The total resistance of parallel resistors is less than the resistance of the smallest individual branch.

distractorRationales.text: It is impossible for current to flow.
distractorRationales.misconception: parallel circuit mistaken for open circuit

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare total parallel resistance with individual branch resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Predict the current in an open parallel branch.

questions.stem: A switch opens one branch of a parallel circuit. What happens to the current in that open branch?
questions.question: A switch opens one branch of a parallel circuit. What happens to the current in that open branch?
options: It becomes larger than the total current.
It is shared equally with every other branch.
questions.explanation: An open switch breaks that branch, so charge cannot flow through it.

distractorRationales.text: It becomes larger than the total current.
It is shared equally with every other branch.

questions.senNote: Checks: Predict the current in an open parallel branch.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Describe potential difference across identical parallel lamps.

questions.stem: Two identical lamps are connected in parallel to the same cell. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: Two identical lamps are connected in parallel to the same cell. Which statement is correct?
options: The lamps have the same potential difference across them.
The first lamp gets all the potential difference.
The second lamp only works if the first lamp is removed.
The lamps must have zero current because there are two paths.
questions.correctAnswer: The lamps have the same potential difference across them.
questions.explanation: Parallel branches are connected across the same supply, so identical lamps have the same potential difference across them.

distractorRationales.text: The first lamp gets all the potential difference.
The second lamp only works if the first lamp is removed.
The lamps must have zero current because there are two paths.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe potential difference across identical parallel lamps.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Choose a circuit arrangement that gives full supply p.d. to each component.

questions.stem: A 12 V supply is connected to two identical lamps. Which arrangement gives each lamp 12 V across it?
questions.question: A 12 V supply is connected to two identical lamps. Which arrangement gives each lamp 12 V across it?
options: The lamps connected with no cell.
The lamps connected only to an ammeter.
questions.explanation: In parallel, each lamp is connected across the same two supply terminals, so each gets the full 12 V.

distractorRationales.misconception: series sharing confused with full branch voltage

distractorRationales.text: The lamps connected with no cell.
The lamps connected only to an ammeter.

questions.senNote: Checks: Choose a circuit arrangement that gives full supply p.d. to each component.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Apply parallel circuit ideas to domestic lighting.

questions.stem: Why are lamps in a house normally connected in parallel rather than all in series?
questions.question: Why are lamps in a house normally connected in parallel rather than all in series?
options: Each lamp can receive the mains potential difference and work independently.
A series circuit gives every lamp the full mains potential difference.
Parallel circuits stop all current from flowing.
A broken lamp in parallel always switches off every other lamp.
questions.correctAnswer: Each lamp can receive the mains potential difference and work independently.
questions.explanation: In parallel, each lamp has the supply potential difference and one branch can be switched without breaking every other branch.

distractorRationales.text: A series circuit gives every lamp the full mains potential difference.
Parallel circuits stop all current from flowing.
A broken lamp in parallel always switches off every other lamp.

questions.senNote: Checks: Apply parallel circuit ideas to domestic lighting.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe series and parallel circuits, junction current, resistance changes, and the core practical using series and parallel circuits.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate parallel circuit readings using current conservation.

questions.stem: In a parallel circuit, ammeter A before the junction reads 0.90 A. Branch ammeters read 0.30 A and 0.60 A. What does this show?
questions.question: In a parallel circuit, ammeter A before the junction reads 0.90 A. Branch ammeters read 0.30 A and 0.60 A. What does this show?
options: The readings are consistent because branch currents add to the total current.
The readings are impossible because every branch must have 0.90 A.
The circuit must be series because there are three ammeters.
The battery has no potential difference.
questions.correctAnswer: The readings are consistent because branch currents add to the total current.
questions.explanation: The two branch currents add to 0.90 A, matching the total current before the junction.

distractorRationales.text: The readings are impossible because every branch must have 0.90 A.
distractorRationales.misconception: total current confused with branch current

distractorRationales.text: The circuit must be series because there are three ammeters.
distractorRationales.misconception: equipment count confused with circuit layout

distractorRationales.text: The battery has no potential difference.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate parallel circuit readings using current conservation.
